firingRanges.name: Raku
firingRanges.summary: Fast, low-temperature firing with hot removal and post-fire reduction or cooling effects.

firingRanges.name: Low fire
firingRanges.summary: Common for earthenware, bright color, and frit-heavy clear or opaque glazes.

firingRanges.name: Mid fire
firingRanges.summary: Common studio range for electric kilns and balanced functional ware glazes.

firingRanges.name: High fire
firingRanges.summary: Traditional stoneware and porcelain range associated with celadon, shino, ash, temmoku, and durable feldspathic glazes.

atmospheres.name: Oxidation
atmospheres.summary: Plenty of oxygen in the kiln. Electric firing is usually oxidation.

atmospheres.name: Reduction
atmospheres.summary: Oxygen-starved firing that shifts iron and copper color responses and supports many traditional high-fire surfaces.

atmospheres.name: Neutral
atmospheres.summary: Balanced atmosphere with little oxidation or reduction push.

atmospheres.name: Post-fire raku reduction
atmospheres.summary: Hot ware is moved to combustible reduction chambers to produce metallic luster, carbon blackening, or copper shifts.

atmospheres.name: Wood / ash atmosphere
atmospheres.summary: Ash deposition and long firing can add natural glaze effects, flashing, and directional texture.

atmospheres.name: Salt atmosphere
atmospheres.summary: Salt introduced into the hot kiln creates sodium vapor and orange-peel flashing on clay surfaces.

atmospheres.name: Soda atmosphere
atmospheres.summary: Soda firing produces sodium vapor effects similar to salt, often with softer deposition control.

atmospheres.name: Pit / saggar atmosphere
atmospheres.summary: Smoke, salts, and combustible materials mark the clay surface instead of forming a classic melted glaze layer.

kilns.name: Electric kiln
kilns.summary: Stable oxidation firing, common for low-fire and mid-fire glazes.

kilns.name: Gas kiln
kilns.summary: Allows oxidation, neutral, or reduction control and is common for high-fire glaze traditions.

kilns.name: Wood kiln
kilns.summary: Long firings with ash deposition, flashing, and directional flame effects.

kilns.name: Raku kiln
kilns.summary: Fast-access kiln built for hot removal at low temperature.

kilns.name: Soda kiln
kilns.summary: Specialized kiln for soda vapor firing.

kilns.name: Salt kiln
kilns.summary: Specialized kiln for salt vapor firing.

kilns.name: Pit or saggar furnace
kilns.summary: Low-tech firing container or pit for smoke-marked surfaces.

clays.name: Red earthenware
clays.summary: Porous low-fire body with iron-rich warmth and strong color response under clear glazes.

clays.name: White earthenware
clays.summary: Low-fire body suited to bright majolica and clean underglaze color.

clays.name: Stoneware
clays.summary: Dense mid-to-high-fire body that usually vitrifies more fully than earthenware.

clays.name: White stoneware
clays.summary: Cleaner background than iron-rich stoneware while keeping more tooth than porcelain.

clays.name: Porcelain
clays.summary: White, vitreous, high-silica body prized for translucency and celadon clarity.

clays.name: Grogged sculpture body
clays.summary: Open, shock-resistant body suited to large forms and some specialty firings.

clays.name: Raku clay
clays.summary: Open, thermal-shock-tolerant body with grog for rapid heating and cooling.

clays.name: Paperclay
clays.summary: Fiber-reinforced body used for lightweight joins and repairs; glaze fit still needs testing.

referenceNotes.title: A glaze system is usually glass former plus stabilizer plus flux
referenceNotes.summary: Most glaze understanding starts by separating silica, alumina-bearing clay, and melting oxides or frits.
bullets: Alumina stiffens and stabilizes the melt.
Fluxes lower the melting point so the glaze can mature at the chosen cone.

referenceNotes.title: Cone numbers describe heatwork, not temperature alone
referenceNotes.summary: Firing rate and soak matter, so the same cone can bend at different peak temperatures.
bullets: Use witness cones to verify what the kiln really did.
Do not treat cone tables as a single absolute temperature.

referenceNotes.title: Glaze fit changes with the clay body
referenceNotes.summary: A recipe that works on one body can craze, shiver, or color-shift on another.
bullets: Red earthenware darkens clear glazes and can magnify craze visibility.
Porcelain shows transparent celadons clearly but exposes fit problems fast.

referenceNotes.title: Atmosphere changes color pathways
referenceNotes.summary: Iron, copper, and ash glazes often look radically different in oxidation, reduction, wood, or raku conditions.
bullets: Copper can move from turquoise in oxidation to red or metallic in reduction.
Iron can move from honey and brown to celadon green or temmoku black depending on chemistry and atmosphere.

referenceNotes.title: Frit-heavy low-fire recipes behave differently from raw stoneware glazes
referenceNotes.summary: Low-fire earthenware glazes often rely on frits and boron, while high-fire stoneware glazes lean on feldspar, calcium, ash, and clay.
bullets: Raw stoneware recipes often need more careful cooling, fit testing, and body matching.

referenceNotes.title: Systematic testing matters more than isolated recipes
referenceNotes.summary: A usable glaze library needs test tiles, line blends, triaxial blends, witness cones, and notes.
bullets: Test on the intended body and thickness.
Record the actual kiln, atmosphere, and cooling schedule.

referenceNotes.title: Surface is chemistry plus thickness plus cooling
referenceNotes.summary: Gloss, satin, matte, crystals, crackle, and variegation do not come from one variable alone.
bullets: Magnesium and calcium often push satin or matte surfaces.
Titanium, rutile, zinc, and cooling profile can promote variegation or crystals.

referenceNotes.title: Older recipe collections are valuable but need modern scrutiny
referenceNotes.summary: Historic recipe pages contain useful formulas, but some use toxic materials, obsolete frit assumptions, or incomplete firing metadata.
bullets: Treat old recipes as research starting points.
Normalize them into chemistry, fit, and safety testing before relying on them.
